NorthBridge Bank — Module 1
Lesson 4 — Monitoring & Observability
The 17-Minute Window  ·  Click any circle to jump

08:51 AM — 17 Minutes Before Crash
Disk Usage Crosses 85% — No Alert Fires
The NorthBridge database server disk usage crosses 85%. A properly configured monitoring system would flag this as a warning. No alert has been configured. The engineer is completely unaware.
What should have happened
Alert: 85% disk  |  Severity: Warning  |  Lead time: 17 minutes
08:58 AM — 10 Minutes Before Crash
Disk Usage Crosses 95% — Still No Alert
Disk usage climbs to 95%. A critical alert should have fired and paged the on-call engineer. Still nothing fires.
What should have happened
Alert: 95% disk  |  Severity: Critical  |  Lead time: 10 minutes
09:04 AM — 4 Minutes Before Crash
Disk at 99.8% — Database Writing Errors
Disk reaches 99.8%. The database begins writing critical error log entries. The engineer has finally noticed — but it may already be too late.
Log entries being written
[09:04] WARNING: disk 99.1% — write degraded
[09:07] CRITICAL: disk full — cannot write
Written locally. Never forwarded. Nobody saw them.
09:08 AM — Database Crashes
DATABASE OFFLINE — Login Fails for All Customers
The database crashes. Login fails for every NorthBridge customer. At 09:15 AM the first customer complaint arrives — seventeen minutes after the first warning signal.
The cost of no monitoring
First signal: 08:51 AM  |  Crash: 09:08 AM  |  Team alerted: 09:15 AM
17 minutes invisible. Zero alerts fired.
08:51 AM — Proactive Monitoring Active
Alert Fires — Engineer Paged — 17 Minutes to Intervene
With proactive monitoring configured, the 85% disk alert fires at 08:51 AM. Engineer paged immediately. Disk cleared. Database never crashes. No customer ever affected.
The power of proactive monitoring
Alert: 08:51 AM  |  Intervenes: 08:54 AM  |  Disk cleared: 09:02 AM
Database never crashes. Zero customers affected.
Monitoring vs Observability
Car Dashboard vs Aircraft Cockpit
Monitoring watches predefined metrics and fires alerts when thresholds are crossed. Observability goes further — engineers can ask any question about internal system behaviour, including failures nobody anticipated.
The analogy
Car dashboard — one temperature gauge, one alert. One metric, one threshold.

Aircraft cockpit — altitude, engine health, fuel flow, navigation, weather, airspeed. Diagnose any problem, not just anticipated ones.
The Three Telemetry Signals
Metrics · Logs · Traces — NorthBridge Monday Morning
Every component generates three types of signals as it operates. Together they give complete visibility — what is happening, what happened, and where a request slowed down.
What each signal answers
Metrics — How much? How fast? How many? (disk 85% → 99.8%)
Logs — What happened and when? (09:04 WARNING → 09:07 CRITICAL → 09:08 ERROR)
Traces — Where did it slow down? (Web 8ms → App 11ms → DB 3,780ms)
The Monitoring Pipeline — Layers 1, 2 and 3
Infrastructure → Collection → Observability Platform
Generating telemetry is only the first step. Signals must travel through a monitoring pipeline before reaching an engineer as a meaningful alert.
The three layers
Layer 1 — Infrastructure: Every component generates signals.
Layer 2 — Collection: Agents forward signals to a centralised system.
Layer 3 — Observability Platform: Telemetry stored, indexed, queryable.
Layer 2 — How Agents Forward Telemetry
Servers → Agents → Central Collector → Observability Platform
An agent is a small software process running on each server. Each agent collects all three signal types — metrics, logs, and traces — and forwards them through Layer 2: Collection to the Observability Platform. Without Layer 2, even working agents cannot get signals to the platform.
How it works — step by step
Step 1: Database Server, App Server, Web Server each generate metrics, logs, and traces continuously.

Step 2: An agent on each server collects those signals and sends them via TELEMETRY FLOW to the Central Collector.

Step 3: The Central Collector consolidates all signals and forwards them to the Observability Platform where engineers can search, query, and analyse.
What Happened at NorthBridge — Monday Morning
Logs Written — Never Forwarded — Platform Blind
Same three servers — Database, App, Web — same three agents, same three signal types. But Layer 2: Collection is not configured. The TELEMETRY FLOW arrows from the agents hit the empty LAYER 2: NOT CONFIGURED space and stop — red X marks block every arrow. The Observability Platform screen is blank. The engineer has no data.
The Monday morning failure chain
Agents present: Yes — all three, holding METRICS, LOGS, TRACES
Layer 2 configured: No — LAYER 2: NOT CONFIGURED shown as empty red dashed box
TELEMETRY FLOW arrows: Hit the empty Layer 2 space — red X marks, no flow
Layer 3 Platform: Blank screen, question mark — engineer helpless
Result: Engineers found out from customers, not their own systems
STEP 1 OF 10
Engineering Principle
Without monitoring, engineers have no visibility into system health except through customer complaints — by then, the damage is already done.
